skill.name: controlnet-pose
skill.description: Pose-conditioned generation on RunComfy via the `runcomfy` CLI. Routes across Kling 2-6 Motion Control Pro / Standard (transfer the motion / blocking of a reference video onto a target character), community Wan 2-2 Animate (audio-driven character animation with pose conditioning), and Z-Image Turbo ControlNet LoRA (pose-conditioned image generation from an OpenPose / DWPose / canny / depth control image). Picks the right route based on video vs still and stylized vs photoreal. Triggers on "controlnet", "control net", "pose control", "openpose", "DWPose", "transfer pose", "motion control", "pose driven", "character pose", "depth control", "canny edge", "use this pose", or any explicit ask to condition generation on a pose / skeleton / motion / depth / canny reference.
skill.summary: Route pose-conditioned image and video generation across RunComfy Model API endpoints, picking the right model for motion transfer or pose-locked image creation.
skill.author: doany-ai

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged 76 patterns including external_commands, network, and filesystem access. All findings are false positives: the bash blocks are documentation examples for the scoped `runcomfy` CLI (allowed-tools: Bash(runcomfy *)), URLs are documentation links to runcomfy.com or placeholder examples (your-cdn.example), and the filesystem reference documents the CLI's standard token storage path. The skill includes an explicit security section covering install safety, token storage, shell-injection boundaries, and outbound endpoint allowlists. No malicious intent detected.

low_findings.title: External CLI execution in documentation
low_findings.description: The skill documents bash commands invoking the `runcomfy` CLI. These are scoped via allowed-tools: Bash(runcomfy *) and the CLI is installed from the verified npm package @runcomfy/cli. The skill explicitly warns against piping arbitrary remote install scripts. No injection surface exists as inputs are passed as JSON strings via --input.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Bash blocks are documentation examples for a scoped CLI tool with explicit safety guidance. No user-controlled shell expansion.

low_findings.title: Documentation links to external URLs
low_findings.description: Multiple runcomfy.com and docs.runcomfy.com URLs appear in the skill for documentation and catalog references. The skill's security section specifies outbound endpoints are limited to model-api.runcomfy.net and *.runcomfy.net/*.runcomfy.com with no telemetry. These are benign documentation references.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All URLs point to the documented runcomfy.com domain or use the your-cdn.example placeholder. No exfiltration pattern.

content.value_statement: Choosing the right pose-conditioned model is confusing when video motion transfer, still-image pose locking, and multi-condition ControlNet stacks each need different endpoints. This skill routes your request to the correct RunComfy Model API endpoint automatically.
actual_capabilities: Route video motion transfer requests to Kling 2-6 Motion Control Pro or Standard endpoints
Route still-image pose-conditioned generation to Z-Image Turbo ControlNet LoRA
Select the right model based on video vs still and stylized vs photoreal intent
Invoke runcomfy CLI with properly structured JSON input for reference video and character image URLs
Document multi-condition ControlNet ComfyUI workflows for advanced use cases
List exit codes and troubleshoot common CLI failures
limitations: Single-condition routes only via CLI; multi-condition stacks (pose + depth + reference) require ComfyUI GUI workflows
Control images (pose skeletons, depth maps) must be generated upstream and hosted separately
Photoreal target faces may need an additional face-swap pass for identity-tight delivery
No built-in pose estimation — tools like DWPose or OpenPose preprocessor are external

use_cases.title: Transfer a dance video onto a stylized character
use_cases.description: A motion designer wants to re-shoot a choreographed dance onto a different anime or illustrated character. Use Kling 2-6 Motion Control Pro with the reference video and target character image.

use_cases.title: Lock a character pose in a generated image
use_cases.description: A concept artist has a specific pose skeleton and wants a character generated in that exact stance. Use Z-Image Turbo ControlNet LoRA with an OpenPose or DWPose control image.

use_cases.title: Build a multi-condition ControlNet pipeline
use_cases.description: A technical artist needs depth + canny + pose all driving one generation. The skill documents the ComfyUI workflows on runcomfy.com that handle multi-condition stacks beyond the CLI routes.

prompt_templates.title: Basic video motion transfer
prompt_templates.prompt:
Transfer the motion from this reference video onto this character image. Use Kling 2-6 Motion Control Pro.

Reference video: <URL>
Character image: <URL>
prompt_templates.scenario: User has a reference performance video and a target character image

prompt_templates.title: Draft motion transfer iteration
prompt_templates.prompt:
I want to iterate quickly on a motion-control composition. Use Kling 2-6 Motion Control Standard (cheaper tier).

Reference video: <URL>
Character image: <URL>
prompt_templates.scenario: User wants drafts and iterations before final delivery

prompt_templates.title: Pose-locked image generation
prompt_templates.prompt:
Generate an image of a samurai in battle stance, locked to this OpenPose skeleton.

Prompt: A samurai in battle stance, traditional armor, cherry-blossom forest background, cinematic 35mm
Control image (OpenPose): <URL>
prompt_templates.scenario: User has a pose skeleton and wants a character in that pose

prompt_templates.title: Multi-condition ControlNet stack
prompt_templates.prompt: I need depth + canny + pose all driving one generation. Which ComfyUI workflow on runcomfy.com should I open?
prompt_templates.scenario: User needs multi-condition ControlNet beyond single-condition CLI routes

output_examples.input: User says: 'Transfer this dance video onto this anime character. Reference: https://your-cdn.example/dance.mp4, Character: https://your-cdn.example/character.png'
output: Intent classified: video motion transfer (stylized target)
Command prepared with --input JSON containing reference_video_url and character_image_url
Output written to ./out when generation completes

output_examples.input: User says: 'Generate a samurai in battle stance locked to this skeleton. Skeleton: https://your-cdn.example/pose.png'
output: Command prepared with prompt and control_image_url
Output written to ./out when generation completes

best_practices: Use a clean, single-subject reference video with no scene cuts for best motion transfer results
Match the ControlNet LoRA type to the control image type (OpenPose, DWPose, canny, depth)
Generate pose skeletons upstream with DWPose or OpenPose preprocessor, host the image, then pass the URL
anti_patterns: Do not pipe arbitrary remote install scripts into a shell — use npm i -g @runcomfy/cli or npx -y @runcomfy/cli
Do not use Kling Motion Control for still-image pose conditioning — use Z-Image ControlNet LoRA instead
Do not use the CLI for multi-condition ControlNet stacks — those require ComfyUI GUI workflows

faq.question: What is the difference between Kling Motion Control Pro and Standard?
faq.answer: Pro is the default for video pose transfer and is recommended for final delivery. Standard is a cheaper tier suited for drafts and iteration on motion-control compositions.

faq.question: Can I generate a pose skeleton with this skill?
faq.answer: No. This skill routes to generation endpoints. Pose estimation tools like DWPose or OpenPose preprocessor are separate — generate the skeleton, host it, and pass the URL to the control_image_url field.

faq.question: Does this skill support multi-condition ControlNet?
faq.answer: Not via CLI. For multi-condition stacks such as pose + depth + reference image, use the ComfyUI workflows documented on runcomfy.com/comfyui-workflows.

faq.question: How is the API token stored?
faq.answer: runcomfy login writes the token to ~/.config/runcomfy/token.json with file mode 0600. In CI or containers, set the RUNCOMFY_TOKEN environment variable instead.

faq.question: Is there shell injection risk in the prompts?
faq.answer: No. Prompts, video URLs, image URLs, and control URLs are passed as a JSON string via the --input flag. The CLI does not shell-expand prompt content.

faq.question: What are the common exit codes?
faq.answer: 0 success, 64 bad CLI args, 65 bad input JSON or schema mismatch, 69 upstream 5xx, 75 retryable timeout or 429, 77 not signed in or token rejected.
